questions.question: What happens when Row Level Security (RLS) is enabled on a table with an active policy?

answers.a: All users can see all rows
answers.b: Queries are automatically filtered based on the policy

explanations.a: RLS restricts access, not allows full access.
explanations.b: The database automatically applies policy conditions to queries.
explanations.c: RLS does not make tables read-only.
explanations.d: RLS does not stop database operations.

questions.question: If two users query the same table protected by VPD, what is the expected result?

answers.c: Each user sees only the rows allowed by the policy
answers.d: Only the admin can see data

explanations.b: Access is restricted, not fully denied.
explanations.d: Non-admin users can still see permitted rows.

questions.question: Which component actually enforces row-level filtering during query execution?

explanations.b: The database engine applies the security policy.
explanations.c: Client systems do not enforce database policies.
explanations.d: The OS is not responsible for row filtering.

questions.question: Why does VPD not require changes to application code?

answers.a: Policies are enforced at the database level

explanations.a: VPD transparently applies policies inside the database.
explanations.b: Encryption is unrelated to VPD behavior.
explanations.c: Users do not manually filter data.

questions.question: What security benefit does VPD mainly provide?

answers.b: Protection of confidential rows from unauthorized users

explanations.a: VPD focuses on security, not performance.
explanations.b: VPD prevents unauthorized access to sensitive rows.

questions.question: After implementing VPD, which action best verifies that it is working correctly?

explanations.a: Table size does not show access control.
explanations.b: Different users should see different rows.
